rules.text: As the day lengthens, so the cold strengthens.
A January fog will freeze a dog.
If the grass grows in January, it grows the worse for it all year.
A green January fills the churchyard.
February fill the dyke, be it black or be it white.
When gnats swarm in February, the farmer takes his coat off.
If February gives much snow, a fine summer it doth foreshow.
If March comes in like a lion, it goes out like a lamb.
March winds and April showers bring forth May flowers.
A dry March, wet April and cool May fill barn and cellar and bring much hay.
So many mists in March, so many frosts in May.
April showers bring May flowers.
A cold April the barn will fill.
If it thunders on All Fools' Day, it brings good crops of corn and hay.
A dry May and a dripping June puts harvest in good tune.
Calm weather in June sets corn in tune.
Sow in the mud and your crop will be good.
Cut thistles in May, they grow in a day; cut them in June, that is too soon; cut them in July, then they will die.
A hot July brings cooling August showers.
If the first week of August be warm, the winter will be white and long.
After Lammas Day, corn ripens as much by night as by day.
September dries up wells or breaks down bridges.
A warm September, a cold January.
If the October moon comes without frost, expect no frost till the moon of November.
A good October and a good blast, to blow the hog acorns and mast.
If November ice will bear a duck, the rest of winter will be slush and muck.
A green Christmas makes a fat churchyard.
If Christmas Day on Thursday be, a windy winter we shall see.
Year of snow, fruit will grow.
Red sky at night, shepherd's delight; red sky in the morning, shepherd's warning.
Rain before seven, fine before eleven.
When the wind is in the east, 'tis neither good for man nor beast.
Mackerel sky, not twenty-four hours dry.
When clouds appear like rocks and towers, the earth's refreshed by frequent showers.
Swallows fly high: clear blue sky; swallows fly low: rain we shall know.
When dew is on the grass, rain will never come to pass.
If the oak flowers before the ash, 'twill be a summer of wet and splash.
Onion skin very thin: mild winter coming in. Onion skin thick and tough: coming winter cold and rough.
When spiders weave their webs by noon, fine weather is coming soon.
A ring around the moon means rain will come soon.
Cows lying down: rain coming round.
He who sows in rain shall weep at harvest.
The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago. The second best time is now.
No bees, no honey; no work, no money.
